data.title: Texas Roadhouse: I Highly Appreciate It, But It's Still Fully Valued
data.description:
Nearly three months after my previous coverage, Texas Roadhouse, Inc. (TXRH) demonstrated resilience after its stock price rose by 4.0%. This is still in line with my cautiously optimistic stance. If you remember, I’ve already noted that it was already fairly valued with a TP or target price of $169.89. Now, the stock is still reasonably priced, supported by its robust performance and well-positioned Balance Sheet. However, it’s still nearly fully valued, while technicals still warrant caution.

TXRH Q3 2025: A Brief Recap

Restaurants continue to face stubborn inflation as their primary challenge. As cost pressures rise, competition and pricing flexibility are affected. This is most evident among quick-service restaurants, or QSRs. Yet, fast-casual restaurants like Texas Roadhouse, Inc., are still positioned very well for the current market environment. Pricing and volume remain well-balanced, allowing TXRH to protect its topline performance.

During Q3 2025, its operating revenue amounted to $1.44B, up by 12.8% YoY from $1.27B. Restaurant sales remained strong and drove its growth as they comprised 99.5% of the total revenue and rose by 12.9% YoY. This also completely offset the weakness in its franchising segment. One factor to consider was its sustained yet prudent expansion. Second was its solid customer base and demand, driven by its value propositions. In fact, its average sales per restaurant amounted to $2.22M ($1.43B / 644 restaurants) versus $2.11M ($1.27B / 600 restaurants). This proves that its expansion remained fruitful.

Q3 2024 Q3 2025
Total Restaurant Sales $1.429B $1.265B
Restaurant Count 644 600
Average Sales $2.219M $2.108M

Meanwhile, its costs and expenses have already caught up with its sales growth due to sticky inflation. This should not be surprising since TXRH heavily concentrates on its owned restaurants rather than franchising, which makes it cost-sensitive. Despite this, the increase in costs and expenses was still quite proportionate to revenue growth. This can suggest that TXRH still remained efficient. As a result, its operating margin remained positive at 6.7% versus 8.0% YoY.

Challenges

As a restaurant, inflation remains a main challenge for TXRH. Although it capitalizes on value propositions, we can see that cost pressures have started to catch up with its sales. At 3.0%, inflation remains stubborn as it returns to its 14-month high. In addition, beef prices have increased substantially in recent months. Last September, the average price of ground beef reached its all-time high of $6.323 per 453.6 grams, or a pound of beef. Another study shows that the price of beef may increase by 60% per pound in 2026. This can weaken discretionary spending and TXRH’s pricing flexibility as it tries to balance demand or sales with costs. It must also watch out for the impact of new tariffs due to their inflationary nature.

Mitigants/Drivers

Despite these potential risks, I believe that TXRH remains well-positioned with some insulation. It’s a fast-casual restaurant, and so its menu prices are set at a premium range. After all, TXRH capitalizes on value propositions rather than cheaper pricing, unlike quick-service restaurants. This gives it some space to cover the rising costs and remain profitable. These are the other strengths of TXRH.

1. Prudent Inventory/Supply Chain Management.

If you remember, Michael Bailen assured us that TXRH has about 50% locked for the fourth quarter. Meanwhile, 40% of its beef is already locked for the first half of 2026. This means that it still has some room to stabilize its cost pressures. It can also delay or at least temper the impact of the rising beef prices. Hence, it can set a strategic pricing to maintain its demand and competitiveness without sacrificing profits.

2. Robust Balance Sheet Ensures Sustainability And Dividend Longevity.

Because TXRH remains profitable, it’s not surprising that it remains very liquid. In fact, its operating cash flow has always been high enough to cover its capex. That is why it maintains an adequate cash level above $100M even after repurchasing shares and paying dividends during Q3 2025.

Also, note that it did not make any debt issuance for 10 consecutive quarters. This suggests that it remained confident about its capacity to generate enough earnings and/or cash to sustain its operating capacity. It also has zero debt, so it remains very liquid.

This is a very crucial aspect since it has a very high concentration of company-owned restaurants. This may also justify its capacity to sustain its expansion. For 2026, it plans to open approximately 35 company-owned restaurants- 20 Texas Roadhouses, 10 Bubba’s 33, and 5 Jaggers. While this can lead to more capital requirements and costs, this move may help TXRH increase its market presence and brand popularity further. After all, the increasing average sales per restaurant I discussed earlier are a testament to its strong demand and brand recognition.

3. Interest Rate Cut May Have Some Positive Spillovers.

The recent policy easing cycle may not immediately affect TXRH because it has zero borrowings. Yet, it may receive some positive spillovers. Lower interest rates can support discretionary spending through credit cards because of lower borrowing costs. Restaurants and retailers may benefit from this trend. In addition, the policy easing cycle may boost borrower and investor confidence, which may lead to increased economic activity. This can improve labor market conditions and wages, which can increase purchasing power.

Valuation

After revisiting valuation, TXRH is still reasonably valued using the Dividend Discount Model. I did not update annualized dividends since the most recent quarterly payout was still $0.68. The difference lies in its dividend growth rate and cost of equity. I included dividends in 2021, so my updated dividend growth rate is higher at 8.14%. Meanwhile, I increased its risk premium to 6.0% to price in more risks and become more conservative. Lastly, Beta increased to 0.95, which means that TXRH became more volatile than in my previous coverage. This slightly offset the impact of lower expected returns. With that, the TP, or target price, is $174.36. This suggests that TXRH is still reasonably priced. However, it is almost fully valued, so upside potential is still limited.

Price ratios adhere to it as they show that TXRH is still trading above the four-year average. For instance, P/E shows that TXRH trades at 26.09x its EPS versus the average of 24.25x. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP will be $158.83. The same applies to P/B using book value and P/S using sales, as they suggest lower TPs. But we do note that the price is still increasing because EPS, book value, and sales are still increasing. Market sentiments are still optimistic due to the resilient performance of TXRH. Nonetheless, it is still almost fully valued. Hence, entering at its current price still has some downside risks.

Technicals

TXRH’s technicals remain weak despite recent rebound attempts. Note that the price is still moving with lower highs and lows, which shows that momentum is still quite weak. The stock price is still below the 200-day SMA. This means that TXRH is still below the average price in the past 200 days. Meanwhile, the 50-day SMA is quite flat, which means that market sentiments are neutral.

Moreover, MACD and RSI are still consistent with the price trend, so we cannot detect any bullish divergence and/or breakout tendencies. The MACD line is still above the signal line and within positive territory along with the histogram. This implies that buying volume is still larger than selling volume. But their downtrend means that selling volume has increased, while buying volume has slightly weakened recently. Its RSI may justify the view since it is still quite near the overbought region. Buying room may be limited at this current level. Hence, I advise investors to stay on the sidelines.

Conclusion

Texas Roadhouse, Inc. remains solid with its robust sales growth despite the rising cost pressures. It is also well-positioned, supported by its prudent beef supply management and solid balance sheet. However, upside potential remains limited because the stock is still almost fully valued. Technicals adhere to it as momentum remains flat. I’m reiterating my hold rating on this stock.

data.title: Rayonier Proves Money Can Grow On Trees, With Potential Merger In Spotlight
data.description:
Today's Pick: A REIT Behind Millions Of Acres Of American Forests

It's winter in many parts of the world, and often this means plumbing issues, so I want to lead into this article with a personal story. While wrestling with a cumbersome bathroom repair/upgrade the other day in a family property, I came to think about all the property maintenance and upgrades that many REITs have to pay for and manage all the time, and so I decided to look for a different kind of REIT for this article, one with no traditional property repairs.

Instead of owning buildings or mortgage securities, have you ever heard of owning an entire forest, literally, and generating cash flow from it?

Apparently, there is money to be made in forests, and a timberland REIT called Rayonier (RYN) has been around since 1926 doing just that.

According to its Seeking Alpha profile, its market cap is +$3B, and "Rayonier owned or leased under long-term agreements approximately 2.0 million acres of timberlands located in the U.S. South (1.74 million acres) and U.S. Pacific Northwest (307,000 acres)."

We can visualize some of that in the geomap below:

A notable piece of news about Rayonier lately is that in October it announced its merger with peer PotlatchDeltic (PCH), as "the combined company will become the second-largest publicly traded timber and wood products company in North America." According to the press release, "PotlatchDeltic shareholders will receive 1.7339 common shares of Rayonier for each share of common stock of PotlatchDeltic."

Since the boards of both companies already approved the deal, I will adapt my research approach to reflect the fact that Rayonier is merging with a key peer, as my understanding of a "merger of equals" is that they are coming together to form a brand new legal entity and current shareholders will have shares in the new company formed. However, I will also consider the risk of such a merger not finalizing for some reason or being delayed, so I think analyzing Rayonier as a standalone company is important too.

One thing's for sure, and that is there sure will be a lot of trees!

My Rating: Hold

After extensive research into this company, my thesis today argues that it is a hold for my first-ever coverage of this stock.

As my rating worksheet below shows, some elements weighing down on this stock being a buy at this time include specific sector risk and technical indicators hurting the buy case, even though there are other value drivers that could come from the potential merger going ahead.

Long-Term Return vs. S&P 500: Both Companies Beat A Key Peer Among Timber REITs

In this first section, let's consider the long-term return of both Rayonier and Potlatch vs. a major industry benchmark like the S&P 500 index and also compared to what I think could be their major peer, Weyerhaeuser (WY), since Seeking Alpha essentially has 3 stocks listed as timber REITs.

The goal here is to gauge whether both Rayonier and Potlatch showed impressive performance vs. a major index or whether their key peer beat them. In this case, although none of the 3 companies were able to beat the benchmark, the data above shows both Rayonier and Potlatch fared much better vs. the index than Weyerhaeuser did, who actually had a -27% negative price return in this same 10-year period.

Now, keep in mind that Weyerhaeuser is not just a pure-play timberland business, as it is also "one of the largest manufacturers of wood products in North America," as its profile confirms, but nevertheless it is the closest peer in this case, and for this reason I am giving Rayonier a neutral score in this category since it did not beat the benchmark, but it, along with its merger partner Potlatch, did far better than their rival.

Sector Risk: Despite Construction Headwinds, Timberlands Offer More Than Just Wood Products

Here, we should consider some sector risks and opportunities in this niche of timber REITs, and what it could mean for both Rayonier and Potlatch, who are combining, assuming the merger finalizes.

One should first consider how a timberland REIT can make money and why. Wood products (housing/construction, furniture, etc.) are one thing, but they can also lease some forests to hunting clubs, sell some pieces of land for development purposes, and also sell carbon credits. So, it can be a multi-stream income, in addition to the opportunity to also get into production/manufacturing processes too. Besides revenue generation, think about the potential for land value appreciation.

Industry group NAREIT said in their October note, for instance:

The sector has seen elevated timberland valuations in recent years, and analysts point to the stability of underlying timberland values as one of the compelling features of the asset class over time.

So, for an investor with a longer-term time horizon, rising land values can drive upside.

The same article also pointed out a headwind: "The slow U.S. housing market is leading to less demand for wood products, and therefore timber."

However, while I agree that can be a near-term headwind, if we consider that future drops in interest rates could spur a construction rally due to lower cost of capital, I think that could drive future upside on the wood demand front.

Also, this could spur demand for more land to develop on. In addition, some data shows continued demand for carbon credits. For instance, the UN Development Programme said this August that "carbon credits are generated by activities that reduce or remove greenhouse gas emissions, such as protecting forests," adding that "interest in carbon markets is rising."

In fact, Rayonier's own projection shows lots of future upside in this niche, according to their last investor deck (pg 38):

Nevertheless, the construction sector is forecast by some to show little to no growth in the near term, with a major financial site citing research firm Research And Markets saying on Dec. 1st:

US's construction industry to record an annual decline of 2.7% in real terms in 2025, owing to contraction across three key construction sectors: commercial, industrial and residential.

Over the remainder of the forecast period, the construction industry is expected to record an annual growth of 1.9% during 2026-29, supported by investments in the development of AI infrastructure.

In this scenario, from peer data below, we can see that although Rayonier and Weyerhaeuser both show negative forward revenue growth, Potlatch is positive, and so I think that strengthens the case that it will only complement Rayonier as part of the new company, and together they will be the key competitor to Weyerhaeuser, as that is the only other major publicly traded timber REIT it seems (although there are likely tons of smaller, family-owned, and non-publicly traded forest lands as well):

Thus, the value drivers here are long-term land value appreciation potential and the demand for carbon credits, and betting on a future recovery of the housing construction market, while in the near term the risks continue to be construction headwinds, so I think a cautious buy is justified in this case.

Company Growth: Value-Add From Merger, But Also Land Appreciation As Standalone REIT

Next, let's consider company-specific growth factors that an investor would keep in mind.

Comparing both Rayonier and Potlatch in terms of revenue growth estimates for the current year, for example, we can see Potlatch is much more positive in that regard, and I think that can only add upside to the potential combined company, since Rayonier is the one trailing behind in this metric:

Further, I wanted to get a sense of whether combining the companies would make more sense than Rayonier simply both being standalones, and the data from the company presentation (pg 13) shows there are significant value-add opportunities to be gotten from the larger company, particularly with greater acreage for non-wood opportunities like mineral resources and solar projects:

I think this added portfolio diversification of what can be done with such lands helps mitigate some of the headwinds coming from lower construction wood demand in the near term.

Now, to add an additional angle to the growth story, let's say Rayonier was not merging with Potlatch. Well, I still would find upside potential in the fact that it has a lot of its land portfolio concentrated in the southern US, and its own investor deck (pg 48) shows that the value per acre in the US South has seen a continually rising trend with a +3.3% CAGR from 2000 to 2024:

While this may be based on past-looking valuations, what I think could drive that trend continually upward in the next decade is shortages of land as population growth puts pressure on the southern US.

Consider data presented by Jaclyn DeJohn, CFP, in the portal SmartAsset, showing 4 of the top 10 growth states in 2024 being in the US South (since 2025 data is not yet complete):

So, there seems to be both value-add potential from this merger going through (added acreage for the combined company to optimize / revenue diversification), but as a standalone company, Rayonier also could benefit from land appreciation itself. For these reasons, I am in the modest bullish range in this category of growth, despite Rayonier's weaker revenue growth estimates this year vs that of Potlatch.

Profit Margins: Both Companies Beating a Key Peer, And Merger Could Drive Synergies

Moving on, let's talk some about profit margins in the context of REITs.

In this case, let's say I was an investor choosing between Rayonier and Potlatch on one side and their competitor Weyerhaeuser on the other. We can see that a core profit margin that excludes depreciation expense, the EBITDA margin, is stronger in both Rayonier and Potlatch than it is in their peer Weyerhaeuser, according to peer data:

This shows that both companies have proven themselves as more efficient at turning a profit than a key peer. Merging ought to bring even more cost efficiencies.

In fact, the investor deck (pg 4) about the merger boasts of potential for "significant value creation opportunity through synergies, operational efficiencies, and sharing of best practices."

Specifically, they pointed to: "$40 million of estimated annual synergies primarily driven by corporate and operating overhead cost savings."

However, as someone who has worked in the financial sector during mergers like that of Charles Schwab (SCHW) and TD Ameritrade, I know personally that sometimes some integration challenges can emerge. So, there is always a risk of higher integration costs and challenges than originally expected. Of course, here we are talking about forestland and not massive trading platforms and financial systems housing thousands of customers' personal data.

So, I think that if the integration process should go smoothly, the long-term bull case can be supported by expectations of cost synergies at the combined company, although even without merging, both companies have shown they can beat a key peer in EBITDA margins.

Dividend Growth & Coverage: Evidence of Diverse Capital Allocation

While Rayonier remains a separate company, in terms of dividend growth and coverage, we can only go by existing metrics, since we don't know what the dividend situation will be at the combined company just yet.

It could be said that data supports my claim that Rayonier has not really been a strong dividend grower over 10 years, with a 10 year CAGR of just 0.98%, vs. the sector average of +1.9%. We can visualize this as a pretty flat growth trend so far:

While Potlatch as a company also did not seem to have impressive dividend growth vs its sector, where I think it could add value to this merger picture is that its dividend payout ratio is just 66%, leaving lots of cash cushion from earnings, while Rayonier's is a whopping +175%, which I think some investors and analysts may consider too high or unsustainable in the event of a major downturn in earnings.

So, that adds to the case that Potlatch can be considered complementary to merge with rather than not.

Further, while Rayonier may not be a strong dividend grower, it is only fair to mention that they have continually been committing cash to real estate acquisitions (driving portfolio growth) as well as share buybacks (supporting the share price), both of which can be upside factors, and Seeking Alpha cash flow data supports this point:

In this scenario, there is value from merging, but also Rayonier on its own has shown that it has committed to diversifying capital allocation towards its growth, beyond just dividends, and for this reason I am compelled to be more bullish than not in this category.

Balance Sheet Risk: Favorable Leverage Picture To Consider, But Forests Have Risks Too

Balance sheet is another category I include in my analysis, and in this case a key element is the debt/equity ratio, so here is a comparison of the 3 peers:

All three peers have relatively low D/E at below 0.60, indicating a low leverage risk in relation to equity, and I think investors who are more risk-averse may appreciate this.

We can see from the investor deck (pg 45) that Rayonier has investment-grade credit ratings from 2 major agencies, which I think is another plus:

When considering the potential combined company with Potlatch, the merger deck (pg 4) says it is expected to have "strong pro forma balance sheet and credit metrics with pro forma net leverage of ~2.5x."

Net leverage can usually be considered net debt to EBITDA, and to use a benchmark to compare against, I would highlight that Corporate Finance Institute says a "low ratio (below 3) is favorable, indicating a company’s capacity to repay debts and potentially better credit ratings."

On the asset side, Rayonier's largest asset exposure is its real estate, to the tune of $2.3B or so as of the quarter ending September, and earlier I said that a timber REIT typically does not have to worry about fixing tenants' plumbing issues like in residential properties, and the land itself (forests) has essentially an infinite lifespan unlike buildings; they still have to manage the forests, and this certainly requires heavy equipment / facilities and access roads.

There is also, of course, the risk of forest fires, tree disease / wood infestations, vandalism, and so forth. This is something an investor should also consider when evaluating the risk of "owning" large forests, particularly millions of acres of them.

So, while I love the leverage picture, when also considering a forest's risk factors, I would be on the low bullish range in this category.

Chart Technical Indicators: More On the Downside Than Not

Even though Rayonier plans to merge with Potlatch, this article is about Rayonier, so in this section I am presenting a very straightforward technical analysis chart, to add another angle to the mostly fundamental analysis so far.

In this example, using a common technical approach like death crosses (sell signals), which I circled in red, and golden crosses (buy signals), which I circled in green, we see recently a death cross happened when the 50-day SMA crossed below the 200-day SMA and then continued its downward slope rather than reversing.

If just going by this chart alone, I would give it a bearish score since it does not show a buy or hold signal just yet. So, this certainly lowers the overall rating for the stock. Of course, I always welcome your comments and whether you agree with my argument that this indicates a continuing bearish momentum.

Valuation vs Future Upside: Undervalued Along With Future Upside Expected

To wind down this discussion, let's touch upon valuation comparisons but also look at some price target forecasts. Although there is a potential merger on the table, keep in mind that it is just board-approved and not yet shareholder- or regulator-approved, as of the writing of this article.

In fact, the company said in its info deck that the "transaction is subject to the satisfaction of customary closing conditions, including receipt of required regulatory approvals and the approval of Rayonier's shareholders and PotlatchDeltic’s shareholders," and is "expected to close in late first quarter or early second quarter of 2026."

So, in this case we will consider how Rayonier's valuation stacks up against its two key peers:

In this comparison, Rayonier as a standalone company (pre-merger) is modestly undervalued vs its two key peers both in fwd P/FFO (a key REIT metric) as well as price/book value. If you are looking for a REIT to snatch up far below book value per share, these are not it, as some of the hotel REITs that I covered recently are. In this example, the market is paying a premium to book value but also seems less bullish on future earnings since Rayonier is more of a pure-play timberland REIT without as much of a wood production business like Weyerhaeuser, for instance.

In terms of future price targets, here is my worksheet to simplify that story:

In the above worksheet, since FY25 is almost done, I used Dec 2026 FFO estimates to get a price target that implies a further +7% upside to this stock's recent share price, while Wall St thinks there is another +33% upside potential.

So, it seems that Rayonier is an undervalued opportunity to snatch up some further potential upside, if considering just this section which adds to the bullish case. However, it did not move the overall needle enough, and so the holistic rating for the stock remains a hold in my view.

Challenge to My Rating: Antitrust Concerns?

While I have not found this to have been raised just yet, in a merger that could create a mega-timberland REIT, with the only other publicly traded one that is a key competitor being Weyerhaeuser, a challenge to my cautious optimism / hold rating could certainly come from antitrust challenges.

Court cases and regulatory fights not only are legally costly can be time-consuming, but also take a saw at the potential merger, potentially delaying it or imposing certain conditions. We also have not yet heard from shareholders, and I've seen cases of activist shareholders trying to block mergers they see as unfavorable, but in this situation I would be more inclined to keep an eye on antitrust concerns.

Conclusion: Agree With Quant Rating

This time, I'm agreeing with the quant rating of Hold, more cautious than the analyst and Wall St consensus, which are both bullish.

Today's article showed that while there are several value-drivers both for Rayonier as a standalone company as well as entering a merger with Potlatch to form a new company, I also showed some key risks that investors should consider when considering a timber REIT.

My call to action in this situation would be that Rayonier present a strong timberland REIT to hold in an already-diversified REIT portfolio with other REITs, to maintain some exposure to the timber real estate sector, which has its own unique niche but certainly plays a key role in the larger macroeconomic picture looking ahead.

data.title: Dycom Industries: The Bull Case Remains Intact
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about Dycom Industries (DY) previously with a buy rating, as the growth tailwinds have great visibility given the record backlog and recurring services. I reiterate my buy rating for DY given the combination of accelerating organic growth, expanding margins, and growth catalysts (data center investments and BEAD).

3Q26 earnings

The bull case for DY just got better with the Q3 results. For a start, the headline numbers were very strong, with DY reporting contract revenues of $1.452 billion, representing a 14.1% y/y growth, which beat the top end of management’s guidance. On an organic basis, revenue growth came in at 7.2% y/y, which was also a meaningful acceleration. Moving down the P&L, the margin results were even better than the top line. Adj. EBITDA grew ~29% y/y to a record $219.4 million, with margin expanding by 169 bps to 15.1%. The point on margin expansion is important because it confirms that DY is seeing operating leverage as revenues scale. There weren’t any drags below the EBIT line, and that led to net income reaching a record $106.4 million, up 34.4% y/y.

Transformational Acquisition of Power Solutions

Back in July, I argued that DY’s move “inside the fence” was the next logical step in capturing more of the hyperscaler wallet, and indeed, management has taken the right steps to achieve this. The $1.95 billion purchase of Power Solutions pushes DY deeper “inside the fence.” For those that are unaware, Power Solutions is not your typical contractor; it is an electrical specialist that targets mission-critical data centers in the DMV region, which I consider to be the single most important geography for AI build-outs. So, having acquired the technical expertise is one thing, but the more important thing is this immediately opens up doors (i.e., now has access to customers' info and relationships) for DY to pursue incremental opportunities.

Specifically, I believe DY can leverage Power Solutions' relationships with hyperscalers to upsell its fiber capabilities, and vice versa. The way I see it, by offering a turnkey solution (i.e., handling both the connectivity entering the campus and the electrical systems powering the servers), DY simplifies the supply chain for clients. And from the customer point of view (especially large hyperscalers), it makes sense to deal with 1 supplier, as they can negotiate better pricing terms. For DY, this approach not only increases the potential wallet share per project but also creates a stickier relationship.

BEAD Program Visibility

I noted the BEAD program as a potential growth catalyst in July, and encouragingly, this is increasingly likely to become a real growth driver. With the Q3 data out, we can pretty much confirm that federal funding is indeed flowing into the system. DY has secured >$500 million in verbal BEAD-related awards, which is the critical signal that convinces me the regulating and funding delays are now easing. The bearish investors would say that these are just verbal awards; nothing is concrete yet, but my view is that when verbal awards reach this scale (literally half a billion dollars worth), it tells me states are moving from the planning phase to actual deployment.

I also noted previously that the BEAD program has a project size of $42.5 billion, but I now think the better number to focus on is the $29.5 billion cited by management. While the overall size is less than the headline $42.5 billion, $29.5 billion is still significant enough to drive solid growth for DY.

With these verbal awards, we can also sort of estimate the timing of contribution. It is likely that these awards turn into actual commitments sometime in CY2026, and if we take the more drastic estimate that it closes officially in 4CQ26, CY2027 should be the year that DY sees an acceleration in growth.

Other bullish data points

At the core level, my confidence in the equity story is still largely supported by DY’s backlog. Total backlog is now up to $8.22 billion, from $7.76 billion in Q4 2024, which clearly shows that demand momentum remains very strong. A good data point that management shared during the call was that they closed another $500 million of agreements post the Q3 quarter-end. That itself implies ~6.4% of growth (which is more than the entire year-to-date growth vs. Q4 2024). This is as clean as it gets to show that demand is accelerating. Importantly, none of these figures include the ~$1 billion backlog that will come with Power Solutions. When you combine DY’s organic backlog with the backlog that Power Solutions brings to the firm, the demand visibility is extremely strong. By my math, the actual backlog is closer to $9.8 billion today (including the $500 million new agreement and Power Solutions), which is more than the entire FY27 consensus revenue estimate.

Now, having a solid backlog is one thing; having the ability to convert it into growth is another. The good news is that DY is incredible at doing so. We can see this from the acceleration in organic revenue growth, which hit 7.2% y/y in Q3, marking the first clear inflection since the start of this year. Note that this was against a high base in Q3 2025, so the underlying strength is real. By my math, we should see organic growth accelerate even higher in Q4 based on management guidance, which implicitly guided for Q4 to see organic growth accelerating to ~15% y/y.

Valuation

I am sticking to my model assumptions that DY can easily compound revenue at 15% CAGR and earnings margin to expand, only that for this update, I expect net margins to expand to 7.5% in FY28. For revenue growth, the organic growth acceleration, backlog, and potential BEAD program award flowing through to bookings are good enough to convince me 15% is doable (note the FY26 guide calls for 15% growth too). For margins, the expansion we see in Q3 clearly shows that operating leverage is kicking in (Q3 adj. net margin was >7% for the 2nd straight quarter). Given this momentum and growth visibility, I think it's fair that DY continues to trade at a premium vs. its historical average of 20x. I value it at 27x, the current multiple, which is slightly above its +1 standard deviation.

Risk

The increase in leverage to ~3x net debt-to-EBITDA (due to the Power Solutions acquisition) is something to watch for. While there is a path to deleverage, which management sets a target of 2x within 18 months, executing this while integrating a large business introduces execution risk. For all we know, integration messes up, and instead of chasing growth, DY has to allocate resources to fix internal problems. This may delay growth acceleration and the deleveraging schedule. Additionally, with ~90% of Power Solutions' revenue tied to data centers, DY is much more exposed to hyperscaler capex cycles, which could be lumpy and make headline numbers look weak in some periods.

Conclusion

My conviction in the bull case has gotten stronger. DY now has accelerating organic growth, a backlog of ~$10 billion including Power Solutions, and a solid path to earnings growth. The BEAD awards catalyst is also becoming more tangible, and I expect to see real results soon. With these factors in mind, I maintain a buy rating for DY as the setup remains highly attractive.

data.title: Active Fixed-Income Strategies: Why Security Selection Matters More Than Ever
data.description:
Bond investors finally have income again, yet the environment heading into 2026 is anything but straightforward. Yields look attractive compared with the last decade, although one leading fixed-income manager recently warned that this is not a “set-it-and-forget-it” market. With valuations tight, idiosyncratic risks resurfacing, and technical forces steering prices, the case for careful security selection has rarely been stronger.

Tight Valuations, Thin Buffers

Credit spreads across investment-grade and high-yield markets sit near their most compressed levels in decades.¹, ² That leaves investors with little protection should sentiment shift. Spreads have far more room to widen than tighten from here, which means most of the return now comes from carry rather than price appreciation. Demand for yield after years of ultralow rates has pulled investors into credit, pushing spreads to levels that barely compensate for taking on additional risk. A broad index today may hold plenty of fully valued bonds with little margin for error.

In this backdrop, selectivity becomes essential. When everything is priced for perfection, the right individual credit matters more than the index average. Several asset managers have argued that success now depends less on owning “the market” and more on identifying issuers still offering reasonable value.

Idiosyncratic Risk Is Back

Complacency can take hold when defaults remain muted, although recent cracks show why security selection matters. A few 2025 bankruptcies—including a subprime lender and a major auto-parts supplier—served as reminders that company-specific failures still occur.³ Default rates in speculative-grade credit have drifted back toward historical norms after the stimulus-supported calm of 2021–2022.⁴, ⁵ None of this signals a systemic crisis.⁶ It does, however, highlight the risk of owning weaker issuers that offer little spread compensation for the possibility of real credit deterioration.

In broad index products, investors can unintentionally hold issuers whose fundamentals have already begun to erode. Dispersion within ratings buckets has widened, with weaker BBB and BB names trading materially apart from stronger peers.⁷, ⁸ For active managers, that dispersion is opportunity. For passive investors, it is unintended risk.

Technical Currents Shaping 2026

Strong demand has supported bonds through 2025, keeping spreads tight even as macro uncertainty lingered. New issuance has been met with heavy subscription, often with minimal concessions. On the supply side, companies have hesitated to lock in long-term borrowing costs at current levels, constraining available paper just as buyers re-engage with fixed income.

These technical supports may not persist. A shift in corporate refinancing needs, government borrowing patterns, or market liquidity could weaken the backdrop. When technicals dominate market direction, passive investors drift with the current, while active managers at least have the ability to steer around excess supply or temporary dislocations.

Why Selection Matters in 2026

Income opportunities have improved, although the easy part of the bond rally is likely behind us. With spreads tight, defaults normalizing, and technicals in flux, security selection becomes the true differentiator. Favoring stronger balance sheets, diversifying exposures, and avoiding issuers with deteriorating fundamentals can make the difference between earning steady income and absorbing permanent losses.

The bond market is no longer a place to buy everything and hope the tide lifts all boats. In 2026, what you own matters more than how much you own. The investors who do the credit work—and sidestep the landmines—stand the best chance of preserving income while managing risk.

Footnotes

  1. Janus Henderson Investors. High-Yield Bonds: Can Tight Credit Spreads Persist? Accessed 2025.

  2. Bloomberg and ICE BofA Indices data cited in Janus Henderson Investors, High-Yield Bonds: Can Tight Credit Spreads Persist? Accessed 2025.

  3. Charles Schwab. High-Yield Defaults: Canary in the Coal Mine? October 24, 2025.

  4. Charles Schwab. High-Yield Defaults: Canary in the Coal Mine? October 24, 2025 — discussion of U.S. speculative-grade default trends.

data.title: Mastercard: Immaculate Execution, Though Not Cheap Enough To Fully Back Up The Truck
data.description:
Introduction

Mastercard (MA) (MA:CA), being used globally by billions of people and one of the 20 largest publicly traded US companies by market capitalization, does not need a thorough introduction.

YTD, its stock price has been ahead of the performance of the broader S&P 500 index (SPX) until decoupling from it in September. MA is now down 5%, while the SPX is up 18%, respectively, though the company has grown and executed steadily. In my case, such situations often present windows of opportunity.

In fact, Mastercard is a Buy thanks to its legacy business continuing to grow steadily (though not rapidly anymore), its value-added services segment growing rapidly and becoming increasingly significant, as well as its historically fair valuation.

Legacy Business is Doing Fine

On October 30, MA reported another quarter of solid growth, at 17% net revenue expansion. Operating expenses grew 2 percentage points slower than revenue, leading to operating margin expansion from 59.3% to 59.8%. Mastercard continues to prove their scalable business model by consistently letting some of their top-line growth trickle down straight to the net income line, which should be welcomed by shareholders.

For Mastercard's legacy business, key business drivers are gross dollar volume, cross-border volume, and switched transactions, which all contribute to MA's top line. All of these showed solid growth, at 9%, 15%, and 10%, respectively. This indicates healthy organic business development beyond price increases, which in Mastercard's case manifest themselves as fee raises.

Another KPI is the number of cards, which continues to grow at 7% p.a. but has seen some signs of slowing down. Altogether, the legacy segment Payment Network grew 12% (10% FXN), which is solid but not rapid anymore. In my view, this business line has a strong foundation and will keep growing but at a more steady pace of 8-10% in the coming years, which can still aid double-digit profit growth due to operating leverage.

Hidden Champion

There's another segment that is much more promising, however. Mastercard's revenues do not all stem from their Payment Network segment. In fact, Value-added Services and Solutions are playing an increasingly important role in their broader operations, now making up around 38% of total revenues.

Value-added Services are growing much faster than legacy Payment Network, and since they function as upsells on Mastercard's already existing payment network and card distribution, they are of a high-margin nature. An increased share in MA's revenue mix is therefore very desirable for investors. This segment consistently grows at 20-25% YoY, just as it has in their September quarter.

Specifically, these services break down into:

  • Cybersecurity and Fraud: This includes Mastercard’s AI-driven fraud and security products such as Decision Intelligence, SafetyNet, Brighterion, NuDetect (behavioral biometrics), Ekata (digital identity), and RiskRecon (third-party cyber-risk scoring). These systems analyze more than 125 billion transactions per year across Mastercard’s network and provide real-time fraud scoring, identity verification, bot detection, and merchant-risk assessment. For many banks and merchants, using Mastercard’s fraud models is more efficient than building internal systems because Mastercard’s models train on global transaction patterns rather than isolated data from a single issuer or processor.

  • Data analytics and consulting: This segment includes Test & Learn, Mastercard Advisors, and the Economics Institute. Test & Learn allows retailers, banks, and digital platforms to run controlled experiments on pricing, promotions, or product changes using anonymized transaction-level data. Mastercard Advisors serves roughly two-thirds of the world’s top 100 banks and a wide range of Fortune 500 companies.

  • Loyalty and Rewards: This includes the SessionM engagement platform, merchant-funded offers, issuer loyalty programs, travel benefits, and cardholder services such as concierge and insurance packages. SessionM integrates customer data across channels and enables merchants and issuers to personalize offers, automate reward triggers, and improve retention.

The advancement of AI technologies will increase their value proposition as insights will be even more accurate and help customers drive better outcomes, thereby helping to attract even more customers AND simultaneously increase customer spend. This results in a long runway for growth in the future and will help margins expand even further as more % of revenue will be of higher margins.

Valuation

In a DCF with 20% free cash flow growth for the coming five years (thanks to revenue growth and margin expansion), 2.5% terminal growth (due to their timeless business model), and a 9% WACC (7.9% derived from FactSet and 1.1% added for safety), Mastercard's fair equity value comes out at $506B, which is slightly above its current market cap of $488B. This goes to show that some future growth is already priced in, though with these assumptions (which I believe are fair), Mastercard is undervalued by 4%.

When looking at MA's historical P/E ratio, it becomes apparent that, at 35, they're not a steal but also not overly expensive, which, at current growth prospects, is attractive.

Against Visa (V), MA looks fairly valued, as for most of the past 10 years, Mastercard has traded at a premium - deservedly so, due to higher growth. In comparison to MA's 16.7% revenue growth, V had just 11.5% in their most recent quarter.

Risks

Mastercard has faced longstanding legal and regulatory pressure over its high fees for merchants. In 2005, a class-action lawsuit was filed against Mastercard (and Visa) by U.S. merchants alleging antitrust behavior. The result was a long legal battle that eventually led to a settlement of about $5.54B approved by the court. Recently, Mastercard and Visa reached a new proposed settlement with merchants aiming to cap interchange fees and give merchants more flexibility. This ongoing dispute signals that fee pressure remains a key structural risk. Because of this pressure, merchants have already started to increasingly steer customers toward cheaper payment methods such as cash or pass part of the fee onto customers, which could suppress transaction growth or acceptance of Mastercard cards. That in turn threatens volume growth. With Mastercard’s valuation already high, modest or slowing growth could lead to underperformance for shareholders.

Conclusion

The investment case for Mastercard remains intact, granting a Buy rating. The stock has not experienced a large selloff, however, and there is no apparent strong undervaluation. Therefore, it is not time to fully back up the truck (yet). Instead, starting to get some moderate exposure now makes sense.

data.title: NIO: Lots Of Catalysts In 2026
data.description:
After years of recording significant losses from its operating activities in the electric vehicle manufacturing business, NIO (NIO) seems to be on track to finally improve its profitability profile. The EV maker issued a strong outlook for Q4 deliveries and drastically narrowed its losses in the third quarter, while guiding for a non-GAAP net profit in FY 2026.

In addition, NIO generated upside momentum in its vehicle margins in the third quarter and reported impressive top-line growth. I believe the guidance for non-GAAP profitability is a major game-changer for the EV enterprise, and with the company benefiting from strong delivery momentum for its new brands, Onvo and Firefly, NIO is set to enter 2026 with a lot of positive momentum. I like that the EV business is focused on growing its margins, and an improving profitability profile could be the catalyst that investors have been waiting for years.

Previous Rating

I rated NIO a strong buy throughout 2025 because I liked the EV firm's momentum in deliveries related to low-cost brands Onvo and Firefly. My most recent coverage dealt with the company's strong Onvo delivery momentum, and the third-quarter earnings report at the end of November showed a rising delivery share of non-NIO-branded EVs. In the most recent quarter, NIO has been successful in further reducing its operating losses amid lower operating expenses and production efficiencies. New models like the ES8 are also promising, seeing stellar product adoption, and could make a positive margin contribution in FY 2026.

Strong Results, Driven by Non-NIO Brand Deliveries

NIO reported both strong delivery and revenue results for the third fiscal quarter on October 25, 2025. The EV enterprise reported total revenue of 21.8 billion Chinese yuan ($3.1B), showing a year-over-year increase of 16.7%. This growth was chiefly driven by a higher number of vehicle sales/deliveries for the third quarter, especially Onvo-branded EVs. In the third quarter, NIO delivered a massive 87,071 units, showing a 40.8% year-over-year growth rate.

XPeng (XPEV) had the best third-quarter performance in terms of deliveries in the industry group, with 149.3% year-over-year growth, while Li Auto (LI) had the worst Q3 delivery results with a drop of 39% Y/Y. Li Auto saw a contracting delivery volume in the third quarter amid growing competition in the multipurpose vehicle segment and suffered a significant, yet temporary, earnings headwind related to a recall that was issued for the company's new Li MEGA MPV.

If we look at the breakdown of model deliveries for NIO, we can see that the EV company started to deliver more Onvo vehicles than NIO-branded vehicles in the third quarter (in August, to be precise), a trend that continued all the way until October. In November, NIO delivered 36,252 vehicles (+76.3% Y/Y), and the EV company has seen a steady ramp in sales of non-NIO brands. In total, 49% of sales in November -- and 58% in Q3'25 -- were non-NIO deliveries, meaning either Onvo or Firefly.

Source: Author

NIO Has Upside Momentum in Margins

While Li Auto recorded a margin drop of 3.9 PP Q/Q to 15.5% in Q3 amid Li MEGA-related recall issues, NIO actually grew its margins to 14.7% (+4.4 PP Q/Q). XPeng's vehicle margin fell 1.2 PP to 13.1% during the third quarter, which means that NIO was the only company in the EV industry start-up group that managed to grow its vehicle margins on a sequential basis in the third quarter.

NIO now has the second-highest vehicle margins in the industry group as well, narrowing the gap with Li Auto. However, the margin drop for Li Auto in Q3'25 is likely of a temporary nature only, as the EV maker has to deal with higher recall-related warranty costs. This means that as soon as Li Auto has resolved its recall problems, the EV company should be able to see an upward bounce in its margins to ~19%.

What stood out from NIO's earnings report for the third quarter was that the EV company drastically lowered its losses due to improving margins, higher deliveries, supply chain optimizations, and growing operating efficiencies (as shown by falling operating expenses, -10% Y/Y). NIO's operating loss declined 28.3% quarter-over-quarter and came in at 3.5 billion Chinese yuan ($495M).

Compared to other EV manufacturers, NIO has the weakest profitability profile, which is exactly why I believe that the EV enterprise has significant upside potential in FY 2026. NIO guided for a positive non-GAAP net profit next year, meaning a fundamental change in the profitability setup could be a major catalyst for a re-rating next year.

From a gross profit perspective, NIO is trailing its EV rivals, but the margin trend for NIO is positive. Further, NIO is seeing surging deliveries of the recently launched ES8, which I will get to next.

2026 Catalysts for NIO

NIO has guided for a total delivery volume of 120,000 to 125,000 units for the fourth quarter, which implies a year-over-year growth rate of up to 72%. The EV company is selling more Onvo and Firefly vehicles, but the NIO ES8 delivery trajectory also looks promising.

Here is how NIO's Q4 guidance compares to its EV rivals:
